chunk_summary: This section discusses monitoring growth, protein adequacy, and bone mineralization in infants receiving expressed or donor milk, emphasizing fortification, nutritional adjustments, and clinical/laboratory monitoring to prevent complications like metabolic bone disease and feeding intolerance.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of monitoring growth parameters in infants receiving donor or expressed milk.
Learn how to assess protein adequacy using clinical and laboratory markers such as BUN and hydration status.
Identify strategies to prevent and manage metabolic bone disease of prematurity (MBD).
Recognize signs of feeding intolerance and appropriate interventions during fortification.
key_facts: Daily weight and weekly length/head circumference monitoring are essential for in-hospital growth tracking.
Target weight gain for very low birth weight infants is 15–20 g/kg/day.
Low BUN with poor growth suggests inadequate protein intake, while high BUN may indicate excess protein or dehydration.
Rising alkaline phosphatase (ALP >800 IU/L) and low phosphorus levels indicate inadequate mineral delivery and risk of metabolic bone disease.
Vitamin D supplementation (400–800 IU/day) and iron are necessary as fortifiers often lack sufficient micronutrients.
Temporary reduction to half-strength fortifier may be safer than stopping human milk feeds entirely if feeding intolerance occurs.
reference_quote: "For metabolic bone disease of prematurity (MBD), trend serum phosphorus and alkaline phosphatase (ALP); rising ALP (often >800 IU/L) and low phosphorus suggest inadequate mineral delivery and should trigger review of fortifier strength and calcium/phosphorus supplementation."
document_key_topics: Growth monitoring in infants receiving donor milk
Prevention and management of metabolic bone disease
Clinical and laboratory monitoring for feeding intolerance
